speaker: interviewer
text: You are now in Rowne, and you had met your cousin Moishe, who wanted to join the Army, and he did.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he wanted to, for the sole reason to take revenge. And he did. And he went in the army. And I think-- [SIGH] yes, I got a letter from him. While I was in Rowne, I thought, I'll visit my hometown, where I was born-- my little town. And I think, at that time, I hoped there were some clothes left from my family that maybe some of the neighbors-- I was going to check with some of these people. And when I came to-- to my town, Targowica--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went-- I went-- part of it, I went with the Russian army trucks. And then I was able-- but just mostly-- yeah, through the army-- through Russian army. That's the only transportation were. And they took me there. There were heading-- I was, like, more or less hitchhiking, while asking these drivers which direction they're going. And they took me there.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was the spring of 1945?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was-- no, this was early in the winter of '45. I mean, that was early-- maybe January, February-- yeah-- of '45. When I got there, I came to the post office first. And I found a letter. There was a letter from my brother, who was in the army in Russia. Sent a letter--

speaker: interviewer
text: Israel? Your brother Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And he-- he hoped that somebody was alive, somebody, you know, in my-- so I was the happiest guy in the world, soon I found out that my brother was alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the letter dated?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was dated-- actually, it wasn't that far, because this is where-- that part was occupied for a very short time. And you couldn't have sent, you know, mail before. Soon you found out that that was occupied by the Russians-- I guess he mailed that letter. And it was laying in a post office. And when I came-- you know, and so I immediately have written to him. And the first thing I wanted-- to go there. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When I came back to Rowne. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] came back to Rowne. I was in-- yeah, they placed me in an orphanage house.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you're back in Targowica, still, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I mean, I went through-- picked up the letter, went back to Rowne.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. My house was not there. My house was dismantled and sold. So all there was-- all I saw is a hole of the basement. The only house was-- I saw, there was my uncle's house was still there. But my house was not there. Yes. And when I went to this veterinarian who lived in a house, I stopped to see them. And I noticed my father's overcoat, hanging there. And, I don't know, we left a lot of things with him, hopefully that he would hide it and, you know, after somebody survives or whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: He said he would?

speaker: interviewee
text: So, I mean, when we left, we left a lot of things to him. So I asked him, does anything from my parents, from our house? And I looked at the overcoat, and he said, no. So.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you say anything to him, then? I didn't say anything. I mean, you know, what's the point? I mean, I saw the overcoat, and he said there isn't. So there's not much I could do about it. He just wouldn't part with anything. So I left and came back. First I came back to Lutsk. Well, describe for me, still, a little bit more, what the town looked like. What buildings were still standing?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans sold a lot of those houses to these farmers. And they took them apart, and they-- you know, this-- and they bought the lumber-- I mean, very cheaply. And they dismantled the houses, and they carried away the lumber of the house. So there was just-- you know, but a lot of houses that were gone-- that were not there. And then there was not much else to do in my little town. There was nothing there. So I didn't stay very long. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] I just needed some clothes, and I had no way of getting it. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I came to Lutsk first, because that was very close to my shtetl. And I met some few Jewish people. And there was one man told me-- I mean, I-- he said he knew that I was broke. I didn't have anything-- no money. And he said to me-- he gave me some money. Just Russian rubles. Wasn't a great deal. And he said to me, he says, if you buy this and take it to Rowne, you could sell it-- and he gave me an address where I could sell it-- and you'll make some money. So with the few rubles, I did what he asked me to do. And then he says, you could buy this in Rowne and bring it here and sell it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's the 'this'?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well-- [SIGH] he-- it was pencils and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] writing paper for children in school. I mean, they manufactured that in Lutsk, but in Rowne you couldn't get it. And he says, if you're in Rowne, you could bring matches and cigarette paper. Well, I-- what he suggested, I did just that. I hitchhiked. I mean, the army trucks, they always-- you know, I never had a problem. They always picked me up-- a kid, you know, they just-- you know. So was no problem there. And I did that, and I made a little money. And then I start wheeling and dealing. I find out my-- and that's how I took off. I ran away from that orphanage house. And I was doing business. I used to bring things and give it-- sell it to mostly ladies who were in this bazaar, this market. And they had stands on the market, and they would sell things. And I would supply them with all the things. At the end of the day, I would come back and collect the money. They couldn't pay me right away.

speaker: interviewee
text: So here I was-- got to be known as a wheeler and dealer. I mean, I had no fear. Everything was black market. There was nothing legal. There were no stores. There was really nothing like that available. [SIGH] And, after a while-- You said something about an orphanage.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I was in an orphanage for about five days. And then I--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they-- somebody suggested I go there. They took me there, and they thought that would be a good place for me. And I thought, god, it's like being in a jail. I mean, I'm already a free spirit. I've been in the open and the out, all these years. And now-- locked up.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the orphanage like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was just-- there were a lot of kids that were pickpockets and all kinds of character kids. And it was just a couple rooms. I don't remember details. I know I didn't want to be there. I just didn't want to be there. There was food. And so I think five or six days, and I just took off. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Then I started doing bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is this still wintertime?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was still wintertime. It was a long, cold winter. I met up with some three women-- three young women. I mean, they were in their 20s. Survivors from-- one of them was from Ostrozec, and she knew my parents and everything. She was in hiding, and she survived. And the other two friends were also from the same area. And they felt sorry for me. They saw me on the market, you know. They asked me-- here they see this kid, and they got to know where I'm from and all that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they said, do you want to come to stay with us? And all they had is, like, an efficiency apartment, you know-- one room. It was, like, three bunks and-- so we took a closet door off and made a place for me to sleep at night, and that was it.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I kept on going from city to city and doing some wheeling and dealing. I was able to buy me some clothes. Most of the clothes you see in that picture of me, with that uniform-- Russian uniform. It was made out of a Russian soldier's coat-- an officer's coat-- that was reworked. So at least I have clothes and food and money that I could buy, mostly things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that the first coat that you had since the war started?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that's right. That was the first one. I was happy. I was dressed like a little Russian soldier. And-- yes. And when I start communicating, corresponding, with my brother, I wanted to go. He was somewhere in the city of Gorky, in Russia. And he says, you can't. I mean, I thought, you know, I'll go live with my brother. But he says, I'm still in the army. You can't go. Then I came back to my little town, because I felt-- you know, I inquired about-- he asked me about my other brother-- if he-- he didn't know anything about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your brother Abraham.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Abraham. And there was another letter from him. So I started corresponding with him. And I got my two brothers together. You know? They didn't know of each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was Abraham?

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was-- he was in the city of Dnepropetrovsk, in the Ukraine.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you locate him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was a letter in the post office.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, there was a letter.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Another one.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. With a return address. But, you know, he hoped that somebody was-- is alive to read that letter. And that's how-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] well, my other brothers said the same thing. I couldn't visit them. I couldn't go to see him. I couldn't go live with them. It was very upsetting, because I thought, you know-- my brothers. And it'd gotten so hard for me to stay there, because they-- they got me-- locked me up on the market, in doing this black market. They took me to the police station with a bunch of other kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russian police. There was Russian police there. Because kids, you know, just doing black market. So they locked me up. And there was, I don't know, about a dozen kids. And they were going to send us to an orphanage house. Well, I took off. There was another kid and I-- jumped out a window and said, the hell with that. And we had the right to register to go to Poland. See, that part was no more Poland-- was Ukraine-- was Russia. And to go further west, since I was a citizen of Poland-- all citizens of Poland, if they want to migrate back to Poland, could do that. Even though you know-- there was-- I was near the area where I was born. So there was actually an order-- Stalin-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Stalin's order, that anybody wants to leave could do that who was a Polish citizen. So I hoped that my brothers would do the same. And we started writing, and he says that-- wait for me. He says, I will also do that. Abraham said that. Well, I waited and waited. They arrested me once, and they locked me up a second time. I mean, you know-- I finally decided that I can't stay any longer. I had to leave. And there was a man who was dating one of the women that I was staying with. And he wanted to take charge of me. He'd lost his family, and he saw that this kid, this-- You know, I'd been drinking, I'd been wheeling and dealing. So occasionally I was doing that. And he just wanted to be-- to take charge, and he'd be like my father. Well, I liked the man. You know, we did things together. But I didn't want him to take over. But he left Rowne the same time. He says, you come with us. I said, fine. We went to Poland together. From Poland, he had an opportunity to go to Italy. And I could go maybe from Poland, later on, maybe to Germany. You know, somewhere out west. From there, you could maybe go to the United States or Palestine-- at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to know how you proved your citizenship, if you didn't have anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came to- yeah. The second time when I came to the post office, I needed a document that I-- that I-- a birth certificate. They didn't have anything on me. So they made-- they have written one up, and they put a stamp. My name is so-and-so, born so-and-so, and that's the only thing I had.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was a birth certificate?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon? Well, it was just a certificate that I was born. I mean, it couldn't be a birth certificate. I wasn't even in a-- it's like a brown-bag paper it was written on, you know? I still remember that. But that's the only thing. But you don't have to lie. I mean, they knew. I mean, normally a kid who has a family is not going to want to leave the family, you know. And I remember there was a line. It was, like, a mile long-- people to register to leave. Of all kinds. You know, anybody was-- Polish citizen.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in Rowne?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in Rowne. And I just went to the front of the line. And I remember, I couldn't even reach the window. I had to stand on something. And, instead of-- people were laughing. They got a kick out of me doing that, I guess. I mean, they just-- you know. And I got my papers that I could leave. All I have is permission to go. That's all. And this man-- Bakalar is his name. And-- and-- and her name is Ronia-- and I, we got to the train-- not 'train station,' but where the trains-- we took a cattle car, actually. It was a freight train going-- facing west. That's how we-- we got onto one of those cars, and the train took off, and we end up going to the town called Chelm-- C-H-E-L-M-- in Poland. We stay there for a few days, in Chelm. Chelm is an interesting little town-- Sholom Aleichem used to write books about Chelm. And I remember, as a kid I used to read these children books. Chelm was, uh-- the fools of Chelm, he would write about. I mean, they do the most stupid things. And here I am in this historical little town. It was overwhelming. It was really something.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we stayed there for a couple weeks. There was already a Jewish organization. They issued me some clothes. I could get clothes, shoes, and all that, that UNRRA and the Joint Distribution was sending in. So I got some better clothes. What was the organization's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Joint Distribution is a Jewish organization. Also, UNRRA was a world organization. They would-- it's survivors, and you know, they were also in DP camps later on. Well, some charity organizations were there, and I was able to get some clothes from them-- and get rid of the Russian uniform-- whatever I had. And they wanted-- Bakalar was going to leave for Italy. And he wanted me to go with him. I probably would have gone, because I wanted to wait for my brothers. They said they're going to come, and I hoped that they would.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I-- I didn't go. All night, the-- you hear them talking about it, and talking about trying to persuade me. And I decided, I'm going to wait. Maybe my brother will come. So I stayed in Poland as long as I could. And things got pretty bad there, too. Where did you stay?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed in different cities. From there I went to a place, Lublin, and I ran into a friend of my brother's, and stayed with him for a little while. From there, I went to another city, called Lodz-- L-O-D-Z. It was a big city. And I stayed there for a while. And I start also doing a little wheeling and dealing there. In fact, there was a point where we used to follow the front line. Another fellow and I-- the older guy. And that was right-- the war was going deeper into Germany. And I would buy-- trade with German people for food. There was no food, so they would-- I would get jewelry-- watches-- whatever-- rings, in exchange for bread and salamis I would get wholesale-- that kind of stuff. And I was able to make a little money.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get the food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, in Poland I was able to buy already food. You know, things were already, you know, there. There was food-- you know, if you have money, you could buy things. Like right now, in Russia-- before, you couldn't buy anything. Now, there's plenty food and things but there's no money.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, by doing that, by traveling on train stations-- I mean on trains-- I used to ride on the roof of the train-- of the cars. I mean, you couldn't get inside. If you can, I was in. I mean, there were just thousands of people were riding these trains.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were always packed. So I used to climb up on top.

speaker: interviewer
text: And hold on for dear life?

speaker: interviewee
text: And hold on for dear life. I've seen some awful things on these. I've seen the Russian soldiers, too, traveling on top. And he was drunk, and he got up under one of those bridges. And he got knocked off. And some other things like that. But you're right. I had to hold on for dear life. And so there's a-- then I found a little buddy, and we both were doing this, going into Germany, selling-- you know, buy things. And sometimes for money-- for zlotys. German-- well, they were not familiar with the zloty. They had the marks, and then suddenly, it's zloty.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we did that for a while. Until there were some organizations forming-- Jewish organizations, smuggling people out of Poland into the West-- Austria, and then later to Germany. So I went with this group who were leaving Poland. From Poland, we went to Czechoslovakia . Is this still the winter of 1945?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is already-- no, this is already-- we passed that. It's the spring and summer of '45.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the war is over.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the war is-- in May-- it was over May the 9th. That's right. The war was over. I was in Lublin when the war was over. Yeah. We saw the war coming to an end and all that. But that was-- May the 9th, it was over. And from then on I went to Lodz and Krakow--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anything special happen that day?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember the Russian-- the soldiers-- there were a lot of Russian soldiers-- was celebrating and all that. Of course everybody was happy. Everybody was happy. I don't remember when Roosevelt died. I think that year. Right before the war or after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or, well, the war with Russia was over differently than with Japan. So I remember there was a sad day when Roosevelt died, because they thought he was such a [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] great person, and he was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] so, where were we? Were you happy on May 9?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, of course I was. The war was over, and I hoped that I'll get to see my brothers. But later on, when I found out that these people are crossing from-- leaving Poland-- and I went with them, I had no idea exactly how we're going to do that, how we're going to go, but there was always some kind of a leader. And he said, when you cross the borders. You don't speak Polish. You don't speak Russian. Don't speak Yiddish, I mean, if you could speak a little Hebrew. Because we're going to leave as concentration-- liberated from a concentration camp, and we're going towards Greece. So we have to act like Greeks, for some reason. When we got to the border, they didn't give us any hardship, because they knew that-- you know, concentration-- mostly were-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and some had these-- still wore these uniform-- these outfits from concentration camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we crossed the border to Czechoslovakia. I was in Bratislava for a few weeks.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the first time I've eaten grapes. The first time in my life I saw red grapes. And I just couldn't get enough of it. So every time we got money, we bought grapes. I thought it was the most wonderful thing in the world. And from Bratislava, we went to the city of Linz, in Austria. And then for the first time, I've seen black soldiers-- black people-- that I've never seen in my life. And that was something interesting. But the-- there was the border-- the Russians on one side of the-- of the river-- the Oder, I think-- and the Americans on the other side. So we were in Linz for a while. From there, we went to Vienna. In Vienna, there was an area where a lot of refugees, Jewish refugees, would be there. And we were there. It's like a-- I don't remember exactly, whether it was like an old hotel, or something similar to that. We stayed there for a few weeks. And from there we went to Germany, West Germany. We came to a place about 30 kilometers from Munich. And they-- that's where, the first time, they opened up DP camp-- displaced-person-- people-- camp. And I was in one of those camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, we were able to go in American Army trucks. They just took us there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's stop right here.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. It's about--
